---
title: "Overview"
---

## Overview

Here's the core problem SLAM is trying to solve: given thousands of noisy 
sensor measurements, how do we estimate both the robot's trajectory and 
the map it's moving through?

The pipeline looks like this:

**Sensors → Measurements → Factor Graph → Probability → MAP Estimation 
→ Least Squares → Sparse Matrix Solver → Best robot pose + map**

Every arrow in that chain is doing real work, and we'll unpack each one 
as we go.

## The Two Halves of SLAM

SLAM naturally splits into two subsystems that do very different jobs:

| Frontend | Backend |
|---|---|
| Extracts features | Receives constraints |
| ICP correspondences | Creates the graph |
| Scan matching | Optimization |
| Visual features | Trajectory estimation |
| Loop closure detection | |
| Data association | |

Think of the frontend as *perception* — turning raw sensor data into 
usable geometric relationships — and the backend as *inference* — taking 
those relationships and solving for the most consistent trajectory and map.

The historical arc of how we solve the backend:

$$
\text{EKF SLAM} \rightarrow \text{Square Root SLAM} \rightarrow \text{Factor Graph SLAM}
$$
